title: Best CRM Software for Landscaping Companies in 2026
excerpt: Running a landscaping business means juggling estimates, crews, recurring clients, and seasonal demand. The right CRM can transform how you manage all of it.
content:
If you run a landscaping company, you already know the drill: spring hits and the phone won't stop ringing, crews need scheduling across multiple job sites, and half your revenue depends on recurring clients who expect reliable service year after year. A general-purpose spreadsheet might work when you have five customers, but once you're managing dozens of active accounts, seasonal upsells, and a growing team, you need a dedicated CRM.

A CRM — Customer Relationship Management tool — centralizes every client interaction, estimate, follow-up, and invoice in one place. For landscaping companies specifically, the right CRM helps you track property details, automate seasonal outreach, and never let a lead slip through the cracks during your busiest months.

Why Landscaping Companies Need a CRM

Landscaping is a relationship-driven business. Most of your revenue comes from repeat customers who trust you with their property season after season. A CRM helps you nurture those relationships by keeping detailed records of every interaction, service history, and property note. Here's what a good CRM does for a landscaping operation:

  • Lead tracking: Capture every inquiry from your website, phone, and referrals in one pipeline so nothing gets lost during peak season.
  • Estimate management: Create, send, and track estimates with follow-up reminders so you close more jobs.
  • Client history: Store property details, past services, photos, and preferences so any crew member can deliver personalized service.
  • Seasonal automation: Set up automated emails for spring cleanup reminders, fall leaf removal offers, and holiday lighting promotions.
  • Revenue visibility: See which services, seasons, and client segments drive the most profit.

Our Top 3 CRM Picks for Landscaping Companies

We evaluated dozens of CRMs against the specific needs of landscaping businesses. Here are our top three recommendations, each suited to a different stage of growth. For a detailed breakdown with scoring, visit our full Best CRM for Landscaping comparison page.

1. HubSpot CRM — Best Free Option for Small Landscaping Businesses

HubSpot's free tier is genuinely useful, not just a teaser. You get contact management, deal pipelines, email tracking, and basic reporting without paying a cent. For a landscaping company with one to three crews, this is more than enough to organize your client base and follow up on estimates professionally.

Why it works for landscaping: The drag-and-drop pipeline lets you move leads from "inquiry" to "estimate sent" to "job scheduled" to "completed" with a quick click. The mobile app means you can update deal status from the truck between jobs. And the free email templates are perfect for seasonal outreach.

Limitations: Automation is limited on the free plan. If you want to trigger emails automatically when a deal moves stages, you'll need to upgrade to the Starter plan at $20/month.

2. Pipedrive — Best for Pipeline-Focused Sales Teams

If your landscaping company has a dedicated sales person or team handling estimates and follow-ups, Pipedrive is built for exactly that workflow. Its visual pipeline is the best in the business — you can see every deal at a glance and know exactly what needs attention today.

Why it works for landscaping: Pipedrive's activity-based selling approach maps perfectly to the landscaping sales cycle. Set activities like "site visit," "send estimate," and "follow up" and Pipedrive will keep you on track. The reporting shows you conversion rates at every stage, so you can identify where deals stall.

Limitations: Pipedrive is laser-focused on sales. It won't help with crew scheduling or job management. You'll need it alongside a field service tool if you want end-to-end operations management.

3. Salesforce — Best for Large Landscaping Operations

For landscaping companies doing $1M+ in annual revenue with multiple locations or franchise operations, Salesforce offers unmatched customization. You can build custom objects for properties, service agreements, and seasonal contracts that mirror exactly how your business operates.

Why it works for landscaping: Salesforce's ecosystem means you can integrate with QuickBooks for invoicing, Google Maps for route planning, and dozens of field service tools. The reporting engine lets you analyze profitability by service type, region, crew, and season.

Limitations: Salesforce is complex and expensive. The Essentials plan starts at $25/user/month, but most landscaping companies will need the Professional tier at $80/user/month to unlock the features that make it worthwhile. Implementation takes time and often requires a consultant.

Quick Comparison

FeatureHubSpot FreePipedriveSalesforce
Starting Price$0/month$14/user/month$25/user/month
Best ForSmall teams (1-3 crews)Sales-focused teamsLarge operations ($1M+)
Pipeline ManagementGoodExcellentExcellent
Email AutomationBasicGoodAdvanced
Mobile AppYesYesYes
Integrations500+300+3,000+
Ease of SetupVery EasyEasyComplex

How to Choose the Right CRM for Your Landscaping Company

Picking the right CRM comes down to three factors: your team size, your sales process complexity, and your budget.

Just getting started? Go with HubSpot Free. You'll have a professional system in place from day one, and you can upgrade as you grow without migrating your data.

Have a dedicated sales process? Pipedrive's pipeline focus will help you close more estimates and track your conversion rates so you can optimize your sales approach.

Running a large operation? Salesforce gives you the customization and integrations to build a system that matches your exact workflow, from initial lead to recurring service contract.

No matter which CRM you choose, the key is getting started. Every day without a CRM is a day you might be losing leads, forgetting follow-ups, or missing upsell opportunities with existing clients.

Ready to Find Your Perfect CRM?

We've done the deep research so you don't have to. Visit our comprehensive Best CRM for Landscaping Companies page for detailed scoring, feature breakdowns, and side-by-side comparisons tailored to the landscaping industry.

title: Best Invoicing Software for Plumbing Companies in 2026
excerpt: Plumbers need invoicing software that works from the job site, handles parts markup, and gets them paid fast. Here's what actually works in the field.
content:
You just finished a two-hour emergency call replacing a burst pipe under someone's kitchen sink. Your hands are still wet, the homeowner wants to pay now, and you need to send a professional invoice before you drive to the next job. If you're still handwriting invoices or emailing spreadsheets, you're leaving money on the table — and looking unprofessional doing it.

Invoicing software built for trades like plumbing lets you create, send, and collect payment on invoices right from your phone. The best options handle parts markup, labor rates, tax calculations, and even recurring billing for service contracts — all without making you sit down at a computer at the end of a 12-hour day.

What Plumbing Companies Actually Need from Invoicing Software

Generic invoicing tools miss critical features that plumbers rely on daily. Here's what separates good invoicing software from great invoicing software for plumbing businesses:

  • Mobile-first invoicing: You need to create and send invoices from your truck, a crawl space, or a customer's driveway. If the mobile app is clunky or limited, it's useless to a plumber.
  • Parts and labor line items: Plumbing invoices need to clearly separate parts costs (with markup) from labor charges. Customers expect transparency, and your accountant needs the breakdown.
  • On-the-spot payments: Credit card processing through the invoice link or a mobile card reader means you collect payment before you leave the job site. This alone can improve cash flow by weeks.
  • Estimate-to-invoice conversion: Many plumbing jobs start with an estimate. One-click conversion from approved estimate to final invoice saves time and eliminates data entry errors.
  • Recurring invoices: Service agreements, drain cleaning contracts, and backflow testing schedules all benefit from automated recurring invoices that go out without you thinking about them.
  • QuickBooks or Xero sync: Your invoicing tool needs to talk to your accounting software so you're not entering the same data twice.

For a full comparison of invoicing tools scored against these plumbing-specific criteria, check out our Best Invoicing Software for Plumbing rankings.

Top 3 Invoicing Tools for Plumbing Companies

1. FreshBooks — Best Overall for Small Plumbing Businesses

FreshBooks was built for service businesses, and it shows. The mobile app is fast, clean, and lets you create a professional invoice in under 60 seconds. For a one-truck plumbing operation or a small team of two to five plumbers, FreshBooks hits the sweet spot of power and simplicity.

What plumbers love: The expense tracking feature lets you snap photos of receipts from supply house runs and automatically categorize them. The time tracking ties directly into invoices, so if you're billing hourly for commercial work, your hours turn into line items automatically. The client portal lets homeowners view invoices, approve estimates, and pay online — which means fewer "when should I expect payment?" phone calls.

Pricing: Lite plan at $19/month covers up to 5 billable clients. Plus at $33/month removes the client limit and adds automatic payment reminders. Premium at $60/month adds project profitability tracking.

Best for: Solo plumbers and small shops (1-5 people) who want professional invoices without complexity.

2. QuickBooks Online — Best for Plumbing Companies That Need Full Accounting

If you need invoicing plus payroll, job costing, profit-and-loss reporting, and tax preparation, QuickBooks Online does it all in one platform. For plumbing companies with employees, trucks to depreciate, and inventory to track, QuickBooks is the industry standard for a reason.

What plumbers love: The job costing features let you track profitability per job, not just per month. You can see exactly how much that bathroom remodel actually earned after parts, labor, and overhead. The receipt capture and bank feed matching mean your bookkeeping stays current without dedicated office hours. And nearly every accountant in the country knows QuickBooks, making tax season smoother.

Pricing: Simple Start at $30/month for single users. Essentials at $60/month adds bill management and multi-user access. Plus at $90/month includes inventory tracking and job costing.

Best for: Growing plumbing companies (5-50 employees) that need invoicing integrated with full accounting and payroll.

3. Zoho Invoice — Best Free Invoicing for Budget-Conscious Plumbers

Zoho Invoice is completely free — not a trial, not a limited tier, genuinely free. For plumbers just starting out or anyone looking to minimize overhead, Zoho Invoice delivers surprisingly capable invoicing without monthly costs.

What plumbers love: Despite being free, you get customizable invoice templates, automatic payment reminders, online payment acceptance (through Stripe or PayPal integration), expense tracking, and time tracking. The mobile app works well for field use, and you can set up recurring invoices for maintenance contracts. For a plumber who just needs to send clean invoices and get paid, it's hard to argue with free.

Pricing: Free for up to 1,000 invoices per year. For most plumbing companies, that's more than enough.

Best for: New plumbing businesses and solo operators who want professional invoicing at zero cost.

Side-by-Side Comparison

FeatureFreshBooksQuickBooks OnlineZoho Invoice
Starting Price$19/month$30/monthFree
Mobile InvoicingExcellentGoodGood
Estimate-to-InvoiceYesYesYes
Online PaymentsBuilt-inBuilt-inStripe/PayPal
Recurring InvoicesYesYesYes
Expense TrackingYesYesYes
Full AccountingLimitedYesNo (Invoice only)
Job CostingNoYes (Plus plan)No
PayrollNoAdd-onNo

Which Invoicing Software Should Your Plumbing Company Use?

Your decision comes down to where you are in your business:

Just started and watching every dollar? Zoho Invoice gives you professional invoicing at zero cost. You can always upgrade to something more robust later, and your invoice history exports cleanly.

Established small shop wanting the best mobile experience? FreshBooks is the fastest path from "job done" to "invoice sent." The mobile app is genuinely designed for people who work with their hands, not accountants.

Growing company needing real accounting? QuickBooks Online is the foundation your bookkeeper or CPA already expects. Invoicing is just one piece of the full financial picture it provides.

Find Your Perfect Match

Every plumbing business is different. A residential service plumber has different needs than a commercial new-construction outfit. We've scored and ranked the top invoicing tools against criteria that matter specifically to plumbing companies. See the full rankings at our Best Invoicing Software for Plumbing comparison page to find exactly what fits your operation.

title: Best Project Management Tools for Construction Teams in 2026
excerpt: Construction project management means tracking crews, budgets, timelines, and change orders across multiple job sites. These three tools actually handle it.
content:
Managing a construction project with spreadsheets and group texts is like framing a house without a level — you might get it done, but everything's going to be a little off. Construction PMs juggle subcontractor schedules, material deliveries, change orders, RFIs, punch lists, and budget tracking across multiple active job sites. General-purpose project management tools can't handle that complexity. You need something built for how construction actually works.

The right project management platform gives your office team, field crews, and subcontractors a single source of truth. When the GC can see the same timeline as the electrician and the homeowner, delays get caught before they cascade, change orders get documented before they become disputes, and projects actually close on budget.

What Construction Teams Need from Project Management Software

Construction isn't like software development or marketing — you can't just drag cards across a Kanban board and call it managed. Here's what separates construction-capable PM tools from generic ones:

  • Gantt charts with dependencies: Construction tasks have hard dependencies. You can't hang drywall before the electrical rough-in passes inspection. Your PM tool needs to model these relationships and automatically shift downstream tasks when something slips.
  • Budget tracking per phase: You need to see committed costs vs. actual costs at the phase level, not just the project level. A kitchen remodel going over on tile doesn't mean the whole project is over budget — unless your tool can't show you the difference.
  • Subcontractor access: Subs need to see their tasks, upload daily logs, and mark work complete — without seeing your profit margins or other subs' rates. Role-based permissions are non-negotiable.
  • RFI and change order tracking: Requests for information and change orders are where construction projects bleed money. Your PM tool needs a documented trail with timestamps, approvals, and cost impact.
  • Mobile access from job sites: If the foreman can't update progress from the job site, your data is always a day behind. Mobile apps need to work on spotty cell connections and be usable with work gloves.
  • Document management: Plans, permits, specs, photos, and inspection reports need to live in one place, versioned, and accessible to the right people.

For detailed scoring of PM tools against these construction-specific criteria, visit our Best Project Management for Construction rankings page.

Top 3 Project Management Tools for Construction

1. Buildertrend — Best Purpose-Built Construction PM Platform

Buildertrend was designed from the ground up for residential construction, remodeling, and specialty contractors. Unlike adapted general-purpose tools, every feature exists because a builder needed it. If you're a home builder, remodeler, or specialty contractor doing $500K-$20M in annual revenue, Buildertrend is the industry standard.

What construction teams love: The scheduling module handles both internal crews and subcontractors with automatic notifications when tasks are assigned or timelines change. The budget module tracks estimates vs. actuals at the line-item level, with purchase order integration so you know committed costs in real time. The customer portal lets homeowners view progress photos, approve selections, and sign change orders — eliminating the "I didn't agree to that" conversations. Daily logs with photos flow from the field to the office automatically.

Pricing: Essential at $199/month, Advanced at $499/month, Complete at $799/month. All plans include unlimited projects and users.

Best for: Residential builders, remodelers, and specialty contractors who want an all-in-one construction platform.

2. monday.com — Best Flexible PM for General Contractors

monday.com isn't construction-specific, but its flexibility makes it surprisingly effective for GCs managing diverse project types. The customizable boards, automations, and integrations let you build workflows that match exactly how your company operates — without the rigidity of construction-specific tools that assume one way of doing things.

What construction teams love: The timeline view handles Gantt-style scheduling with dependencies. The dashboards pull data across multiple projects so you can see your entire portfolio's status at a glance. Automations can trigger notifications when budgets hit thresholds, when tasks are overdue, or when subs haven't updated their status. The file management column on any board item means plans, photos, and documents attach directly to the relevant task. And the integration library connects monday.com to QuickBooks, Procore, Google Drive, and hundreds of other tools your team already uses.

Pricing: Basic at $12/seat/month, Standard at $14/seat/month (adds timeline and Gantt), Pro at $28/seat/month (adds time tracking and workload management).

Best for: General contractors and commercial construction firms that need flexibility across project types.

3. Asana — Best for Small Crews and Residential Subcontractors

For smaller construction operations — a three-person framing crew, a residential electrical contractor, a small plumbing outfit — Asana provides clean task management without the overhead of enterprise construction platforms. You don't need Buildertrend's feature depth if you're running two to three jobs at a time. You need clarity on what's due, what's done, and what's blocked.

What construction teams love: The free tier supports up to 15 users with unlimited projects. Task templates let you create repeatable checklists for common jobs — rough-in, trim-out, punch list — so nothing gets missed. The mobile app is fast and works well for field updates. The timeline view on paid plans gives you basic Gantt functionality. And the simplicity means your crew will actually use it, which is half the battle with any PM tool.

Pricing: Free for up to 15 users. Premium at $13/user/month adds timelines, custom fields, and dashboards. Business at $30/user/month adds portfolios and workload management.

Best for: Small crews and specialty subcontractors who need task management without enterprise complexity.

Feature Comparison

FeatureBuildertrendmonday.comAsana
Starting Price$199/month$12/seat/monthFree
Construction-SpecificYesNo (customizable)No
Gantt/TimelineYesYes (Standard+)Yes (Premium+)
Budget TrackingBuilt-inCustom columnsCustom fields
Change Order TrackingBuilt-inCustomizableManual
Client PortalYesNoNo
Sub AccessBuilt-inGuest seatsGuest seats
Daily LogsBuilt-inCustom formsCustom forms
Mobile AppGoodGoodExcellent

Making the Right Choice for Your Construction Business

Running residential builds or remodels? Buildertrend is the obvious choice. It's built for your exact workflow, and the customer portal alone will save you hours of back-and-forth per project.

Managing diverse commercial projects? monday.com's flexibility lets you adapt your project management to each project type rather than forcing every job into the same template.

Small crew keeping it simple? Asana's free tier gives you everything you need to stay organized across a few active jobs without paying for features you'll never use.

Explore the Full Rankings

We've tested and scored project management tools specifically for construction use cases — scheduling crews, tracking budgets, managing subs, and handling change orders. See how these tools stack up across every feature that matters at our Best Project Management for Construction comparison page.

title: HubSpot vs Pipedrive: Which CRM is Right for Your Small Business?
excerpt: HubSpot and Pipedrive are two of the most popular CRMs for small businesses, but they solve different problems. Here's an honest breakdown of when each one wins.
content:
If you've been researching CRMs for your small business, HubSpot and Pipedrive probably keep appearing at the top of every list. Both are excellent products, but they're built for different priorities. HubSpot wants to be your entire growth platform — CRM, marketing, sales, and service in one ecosystem. Pipedrive wants to be the best sales pipeline tool on the market, period. That fundamental difference shapes everything from pricing to daily usability.

This isn't a "HubSpot is better" or "Pipedrive is better" article. The right answer depends entirely on how your business sells, what you need beyond sales tracking, and how much you're willing to pay as you scale. Let's break it down honestly.

The Core Difference

HubSpot is a platform. Its CRM is the foundation for a suite that includes marketing automation, email campaigns, landing pages, customer service ticketing, and content management. You can start with the free CRM and add hubs as you grow. The trade-off is complexity — there's a lot to learn, and the paid tiers get expensive fast.

Pipedrive is a pipeline tool. It does one thing — manage your sales process — and does it exceptionally well. The interface is built around a visual pipeline that makes it immediately obvious where every deal stands and what action is needed next. The trade-off is scope — if you need marketing automation or service ticketing, you'll need separate tools.

Head-to-Head Comparison

CategoryHubSpotPipedriveWinner
Free PlanGenerous (CRM, forms, email)No free plan (14-day trial)HubSpot
Paid Starting Price$20/month (Starter)$14/user/month (Essential)Pipedrive
Pipeline VisualizationGoodExcellent — best in classPipedrive
Email MarketingBuilt-in (Marketing Hub)Basic (add-on Campaigns)HubSpot
Marketing AutomationPowerful (Professional tier)LimitedHubSpot
Ease of UseModerate learning curveVery intuitivePipedrive
ReportingComprehensiveSales-focusedHubSpot
Mobile AppFull-featuredExcellent for salesTie
Integrations1,500+ in marketplace400+ in marketplaceHubSpot
ScalabilityEnterprise-readyMid-market ceilingHubSpot
Price at ScaleGets expensive ($800+/mo)Stays reasonablePipedrive
Setup TimeHours to daysUnder an hourPipedrive

When HubSpot Wins

You Need Marketing and Sales in One Place

If your business relies on inbound leads — blog content, landing pages, email nurture sequences, and form submissions — HubSpot's integration between marketing and sales is unmatched. A lead fills out a form, enters a nurture sequence, gets scored based on engagement, and surfaces in your sales pipeline at exactly the right moment. Building that workflow across separate tools is possible but painful.

You're a Team of One (Right Now)

HubSpot's free CRM is the best free option available. You get contact management, deal tracking, email templates, meeting scheduling, and basic reporting without paying anything. For a solo business owner who needs to get organized before committing to a monthly expense, this is the smart starting point.

You Want a Platform to Grow Into

HubSpot's ecosystem means you can add marketing automation, service ticketing, CMS, and operations tools without switching platforms or losing data. If you're planning to build a marketing-driven growth engine over the next few years, starting on HubSpot avoids a painful migration later.

When Pipedrive Wins

You Have a Clear Sales Process

If your business grows by actively selling — cold outreach, demos, follow-ups, proposals — Pipedrive is purpose-built for that motion. The visual pipeline makes it impossible to lose track of a deal. Activity-based reminders ensure every prospect gets the right follow-up at the right time. Your team spends time selling, not navigating software.

Your Team Needs to Adopt It Fast

Pipedrive's learning curve is nearly flat. New salespeople can be productive within hours, not days. For small teams where everyone wears multiple hats and nobody has time for CRM training, this adoption speed is a genuine competitive advantage. The best CRM is the one your team actually uses.

You Want Predictable Costs

Pipedrive's pricing stays reasonable as you grow. The Professional plan at $49/user/month includes everything most small businesses need — automation, email sequences, revenue forecasting, and custom reporting. Meanwhile, HubSpot's equivalent features require the Professional tier at $500+/month, which is a significant jump from the Starter plan.

Industry-Specific Considerations

The right CRM also depends on your industry. Service businesses like landscaping companies, plumbing operations, and contractors have different needs than SaaS companies or agencies.

For industries with long relationship cycles and seasonal demand, HubSpot's marketing automation helps maintain engagement during slow periods. For a deeper analysis, see our recommendations for the best CRM for landscaping companies and the best CRM for plumbing companies.

For businesses where the sales process is the primary growth lever — you're actively prospecting, quoting, and closing — Pipedrive's pipeline focus will serve you better than HubSpot's broader but shallower sales features.

The Pricing Reality Check

This is where the decision gets real. Here's what each platform costs for a team of 5 with the features most small businesses need (automation, reporting, email sequences):

  • HubSpot: Marketing Hub Starter ($20/mo) + Sales Hub Professional ($500/mo for 5 users) = $520/month minimum for full functionality.
  • Pipedrive: Professional plan at $49/user × 5 users = $245/month. Add Campaigns add-on for basic email marketing at $16/month = $261/month total.

Pipedrive delivers comparable sales functionality at roughly half the cost of HubSpot's paid tiers. But if you genuinely need HubSpot's marketing automation depth — lead scoring, multi-touch attribution, A/B testing on email sequences — there's no Pipedrive equivalent. You'd need to add a separate tool like Mailchimp or ActiveCampaign, which adds complexity and cost.

Our Recommendation

Choose HubSpot if: Your growth depends on inbound marketing, you want an all-in-one platform, or you're starting with the free tier and plan to invest in marketing automation later.

Choose Pipedrive if: Your growth depends on outbound sales execution, you want your team productive immediately, or you need strong pipeline management at a reasonable price.

For a detailed, scored comparison of these two CRMs across features that matter to small businesses, visit our full HubSpot vs Pipedrive comparison page.

title: Best Accounting Software for Restaurants in 2026
excerpt: Restaurant accounting isn't like other small business accounting. Between food costs, tip reporting, and razor-thin margins, you need software that gets the industry.
content:
Restaurant accounting is uniquely painful. You're tracking food costs that fluctuate weekly, managing tip pools and tip credits across a staff that turns over constantly, reconciling POS transactions with bank deposits that never quite match, handling sales tax on some items but not others, and doing all of this on margins so thin that a 2% food cost overrun can wipe out your monthly profit. QuickBooks tutorials designed for consultants and freelancers don't prepare you for any of that.

The right accounting software for a restaurant doesn't just record transactions — it helps you understand your prime cost (food + labor as a percentage of revenue), track waste, manage vendor payments, and generate the reports your accountant needs without you spending Sunday mornings in the back office. Here's what to look for and which tools do it best.

What Restaurant Accounting Software Needs to Handle

Before we get to specific tools, let's be clear about what makes restaurant accounting different from general small business accounting:

  • Food cost tracking: Your cost of goods sold changes every week based on supplier pricing, seasonal availability, and waste. You need to track food cost as a percentage of revenue and spot trends before they eat your margins.
  • Tip management: Between tip pools, tip credits, tip reporting for tax purposes, and the various state laws governing all of it, tips are an accounting nightmare. Your software needs to handle tip allocation and generate the right tax documents.
  • POS integration: Your point-of-sale system is the source of truth for daily revenue. If your accounting software doesn't sync with your POS, you're manually entering sales data every day — or worse, every week — and errors compound quickly.
  • Payroll complexity: Restaurant payroll involves tipped minimum wage, overtime across fluctuating schedules, and sometimes multiple pay rates for the same employee (server wage vs. prep cook wage for someone who does both).
  • Vendor management: Restaurants work with dozens of vendors — food distributors, beverage suppliers, linen services, equipment maintenance. Tracking payables, managing payment terms, and categorizing expenses correctly is critical for understanding your true costs.
  • Multi-location reporting: If you operate more than one location, you need to see each store's P&L independently while also rolling up to a consolidated view.

For our full scored rankings of accounting tools evaluated against these restaurant-specific criteria, visit our Best Accounting Software for Restaurants page.

Top 3 Accounting Tools for Restaurants

1. QuickBooks Online — Best All-Around Choice for Most Restaurants

QuickBooks Online is the default accounting platform for small businesses in the US, and restaurants are no exception. Its strength is ecosystem — the integration library includes every major restaurant POS (Toast, Square, Clover, Lightspeed), payroll services, and inventory tools. Your accountant almost certainly knows QuickBooks, which means less explaining and faster tax prep.

What restaurants love: The bank feed matching automates daily reconciliation. You connect your business bank account and credit cards, and QuickBooks matches transactions to POS deposits, vendor payments, and payroll automatically. The class and location tracking features let you tag every transaction by revenue center (dine-in, takeout, catering, bar) and by location if you have multiple stores. The profit-and-loss reports can break down by class and location, giving you the granular visibility restaurants need. The built-in payroll add-on handles tipped employees, though you'll want to verify it meets your state's specific tip credit rules.

Pricing: Simple Start at $30/month, Essentials at $60/month, Plus at $90/month (recommended for restaurants — adds class tracking and inventory). Payroll is an additional $50-$125/month depending on tier.

Best for: Single and multi-location restaurants that want the most accountant-friendly, integration-rich platform.

2. Xero — Best for Multi-Location Restaurant Groups

Xero has gained significant ground in the restaurant industry, particularly among multi-unit operators. Its clean interface, strong multi-currency support, and unlimited user seats on every plan make it compelling for growing restaurant groups where multiple managers and an outsourced bookkeeper all need access.

What restaurants love: Unlimited users on every plan is a standout feature for restaurants where the GM, chef, bar manager, and external accountant all need some level of financial visibility. The tracking categories work similarly to QuickBooks classes but are arguably easier to set up and filter by. Xero's bank reconciliation is fast and accurate, and the POS integrations (via apps like Shogo, Dext, and direct integrations with Square and Lightspeed) handle daily sales import well. The accounts payable workflow is particularly strong — you can upload vendor invoices, match them to purchase orders, and schedule payments in batches.

Pricing: Starter at $29/month (limited to 20 invoices), Standard at $46/month, Premium at $62/month (adds multi-currency and project tracking).

Best for: Restaurant groups with multiple locations, multiple stakeholders needing access, and complex vendor management.

3. FreshBooks — Best for Small Owner-Operated Restaurants

FreshBooks isn't the first name you'd think of for restaurant accounting, but for small, owner-operated establishments — a family pizza shop, a food truck, a small café — it offers the simplest path from chaos to organized finances. If you're currently doing your books in a shoebox or a spreadsheet, FreshBooks will feel like a massive upgrade without the learning curve of QuickBooks or Xero.

What restaurants love: The expense tracking with receipt capture is ideal for owners who do their own supply runs. Snap a photo of the Costco receipt, categorize it, and it's in your books. The invoicing features are useful for catering orders or private events where you need to bill after the fact. The profit-and-loss reports are straightforward and easy to understand for non-accountants. And the time tracking can be useful for tracking prep hours if you bill differently for catering labor.

Pricing: Lite at $19/month, Plus at $33/month, Premium at $60/month.

Best for: Small, owner-operated restaurants and food trucks that need simple, clean accounting without enterprise complexity.

Comparison Table

FeatureQuickBooks OnlineXeroFreshBooks
Starting Price$30/month$29/month$19/month
POS IntegrationsExtensive (Toast, Square, Clover)Good (Square, Lightspeed, via apps)Limited (Square)
Users Included1-25 (varies by plan)Unlimited1-5 (varies by plan)
Tip TrackingVia payroll add-onVia Gusto/other payrollLimited
Class/Location TrackingYes (Plus plan)Yes (all plans)No
Inventory TrackingBasic (Plus plan)Via add-onsNo
Built-in PayrollYes (add-on)No (integrations)No
Multi-LocationYesYesLimited
Accountant AccessFree accountant loginFree advisor loginFree accountant login

Choosing the Right Accounting Software for Your Restaurant

Single location, want industry-standard reliability? QuickBooks Online Plus with the payroll add-on gives you the most complete restaurant accounting stack with the widest accountant compatibility.

Multi-location group with many stakeholders? Xero's unlimited users and clean multi-entity reporting make it the better fit for growing restaurant groups.

Small operation, first time doing real books? FreshBooks gets you organized fast with the least friction. You can always graduate to QuickBooks or Xero when your complexity demands it.

See the Full Rankings

We've evaluated accounting software specifically through the lens of restaurant operations — POS integration, food cost tracking, tip management, and multi-location reporting. For detailed scoring and additional options, visit our Best Accounting Software for Restaurants comparison page.

title: Best Email Marketing Software for Real Estate Agents in 2026
excerpt: Real estate agents live and die by their contact list. The right email marketing tool turns past clients into referrals and cold leads into closings.
content:
In real estate, your database is your business. Every past client, open house visitor, expired listing lead, and sphere-of-influence contact represents potential commission — but only if you stay top of mind. The agents who consistently earn $200K+ aren't necessarily better at showing homes. They're better at systematic follow-up. And in 2026, that means email marketing.

But real estate email marketing isn't like e-commerce blasts or SaaS newsletters. You're nurturing relationships that might take 18 months to convert. You're sending hyper-local market updates that need to feel personal, not automated. You're managing drip campaigns for buyers and sellers simultaneously, and you need to showcase listings with beautiful visuals that work on every device. Generic email tools can do some of this, but the best platforms for real estate make it effortless.

What Real Estate Agents Need from Email Marketing Software

Before diving into specific tools, here's what separates a good email marketing platform from a great one for real estate agents:

  • Contact segmentation: You need to send different content to buyers vs. sellers, hot leads vs. nurture contacts, past clients vs. prospects, and geographic farm areas. The ability to segment and tag contacts precisely determines whether your emails feel relevant or spammy.
  • Drip campaign automation: A new lead from Zillow should trigger a 12-touch sequence over 90 days. A past client should get a home anniversary email, a market update quarterly, and a check-in every six months. These sequences need to run on autopilot once you set them up.
  • Listing-friendly templates: Real estate emails need to showcase properties with large images, virtual tour links, and clean layouts. Templates designed for listing announcements, open house invites, just-sold announcements, and market reports save you hours of design time.
  • CRM integration: Your email platform needs to talk to your CRM (or have one built in) so that contact activity, email opens, and link clicks feed back into your lead scoring and follow-up priorities.
  • Deliverability: None of this matters if your emails land in spam. Platforms with strong sender reputation management and authentication features keep your messages in the primary inbox.
  • Compliance: Real estate email marketing must comply with CAN-SPAM (and state-specific rules). Built-in unsubscribe management, physical address inclusion, and opt-in tracking keep you legal.

For scored rankings of email marketing tools against these real estate-specific criteria, check our Best Email Marketing for Real Estate comparison page.

Top 3 Email Marketing Tools for Real Estate Agents

1. Mailchimp — Best All-Around Email Marketing for Solo Agents and Small Teams

Mailchimp has evolved far beyond its roots as a simple newsletter tool. The platform now includes automation journeys, landing pages, audience segmentation, and predictive analytics. For real estate agents, Mailchimp offers the best balance of power, usability, and price — especially if you're managing your own marketing without a dedicated marketing coordinator.

What real estate agents love: The Customer Journey Builder lets you create sophisticated drip campaigns with branching logic — if a lead opens your listing alert, send a showing invitation; if they don't, send a different subject line three days later. The content studio stores your listing photos and generates mobile-optimized layouts automatically. Audience segmentation lets you tag contacts by buyer/seller status, price range, neighborhood interest, and lead source so every email feels targeted. The free tier supports up to 500 contacts with basic automation, which is enough for agents just building their database.

Real estate workflow example: A lead visits your website and downloads a neighborhood guide (captured via Mailchimp landing page). They're automatically tagged as a buyer interested in that neighborhood and enter a 10-email drip campaign that includes market stats, new listings in the area, testimonials from past buyers, and a CTA to schedule a consultation. If they click on a listing link, they get re-tagged as "active buyer" and you get notified to follow up personally.

Pricing: Free for up to 500 contacts. Essentials at $13/month for 500 contacts. Standard at $20/month adds automation journeys and predictive segmentation. Premium at $350/month for advanced enterprise features.

Best for: Solo agents and small teams who want powerful automation without needing a marketing degree.

2. Constant Contact — Best for Agents Who Want Simplicity and Support

Constant Contact has long been a favorite in real estate because it's genuinely easy to use. The drag-and-drop editor, pre-built real estate templates, and responsive phone support make it the right choice for agents who want email marketing to work without becoming email marketing experts. If you've tried Mailchimp and found it overwhelming, Constant Contact is your answer.

What real estate agents love: The real estate-specific template library includes designs for just-listed, just-sold, open house, market update, and holiday greeting emails — all professionally designed and mobile responsive. The event management feature is surprisingly useful for open houses — you can send invitations, track RSVPs, and follow up with attendees automatically. The social media posting feature lets you share your email content to Facebook and Instagram simultaneously. And the contact management automatically cleans your list by removing bounces and unsubscribes, which protects your deliverability over time.

Real estate workflow example: You host an open house and collect contact information via a Constant Contact sign-up form on your tablet. Visitors are automatically added to a "Open House - 123 Main St" segment and receive a follow-up email the next morning thanking them for visiting, with links to the listing, a virtual tour, and your contact information. Those who click the virtual tour link get a second follow-up three days later asking if they'd like a private showing.

Pricing: Lite at $12/month for up to 500 contacts. Standard at $35/month adds automation and segmentation. Premium at $80/month adds advanced automation and reporting.

Best for: Agents who want email marketing to "just work" with real estate-ready templates and hands-on support.

3. HubSpot — Best for Teams Building a Full Marketing Engine

HubSpot's Marketing Hub goes well beyond email — it's a complete inbound marketing platform with landing pages, blog hosting, SEO tools, social media management, and lead scoring built in. For real estate teams or brokerages that want to build a content-driven lead generation machine, HubSpot provides the infrastructure to execute at scale.

What real estate agents love: The CRM is built in and free, so every email interaction feeds directly into your contact record. Lead scoring assigns points based on email opens, link clicks, website visits, and form submissions — so you know which leads are actively house-hunting right now. The smart content feature lets you show different email content to different segments within the same email — first-time buyers see starter home tips while move-up buyers see market appreciation data. The attribution reporting shows which email campaigns actually generate clients, not just opens.

Real estate workflow example: Your brokerage blog post about "Top 10 Neighborhoods for Families in Austin" ranks on Google and drives traffic. Visitors who subscribe to your newsletter via HubSpot form enter a nurture sequence. HubSpot tracks their website behavior — which listing pages they visit, which neighborhood guides they download — and progressively scores them. When a lead hits a score threshold, the assigned agent gets an alert with a complete activity timeline, making the first call informed and relevant rather than cold.

Pricing: Free CRM and basic email. Marketing Hub Starter at $20/month. Professional at $890/month adds full automation, smart content, and attribution reporting.

Best for: Real estate teams and brokerages building a content-and-email lead generation system.

Which Email Marketing Tool Should You Choose?

Solo agent building a database? Start with Mailchimp's free tier. You'll have automation and segmentation capabilities that grow with you, and the learning curve is manageable.

Want simplicity and real estate templates? Constant Contact is the path of least resistance. You'll be sending professional listing emails and open house invitations within an hour of signing up.

Running a team or brokerage? HubSpot's integrated CRM and marketing platform gives you the infrastructure for scalable lead generation, though the Professional tier is a significant investment.
